input: Prof. Smith works at a large university. At this university, in order to get new computers from the university, faculty like Prof. Smith must send an application to two administrative committees, the IT committee and the department budget committee. Prof. Smith will be able to get her new computers if the IT committee approves her application or the department budget committee approves her application. Only one of the committees needs to approve her application for her to get the new computers. The IT committee almost always approves these applications. The department budget committee also almost always approves these applications. The budget committee is notorious for approving almost every application they receive. Prof. Smith sends in her applications. Each committee meets independently and they decide without talking to each other, but their meetings are scheduled for the exact same time. The IT committee approves her application, and as expected, the department budget committee approves her application. So, Prof. Smith got her new computers. Does the department budget committee cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application?
debate_transcript: [Ordinary_Citizen]: Prof. Smith works at a large university. At this university, in order to get new computers from the university, faculty like Prof. Smith must send an application to two administrative committees, the IT committee and the department budget committee. Prof. Smith will be able to get her new computers if the IT committee approves her application or the department budget committee approves her application. Only one of the committees needs to approve her application for her to get the new computers. The IT committee almost always approves these applications. The department budget committee also almost always approves these applications. The budget committee is notorious for approving almost every application they receive. Prof. Smith sends in her applications. Each committee meets independently and they decide without talking to each other, but their meetings are scheduled for the exact same time. The IT committee approves her application, and as expected, the department budget committee approves her application. So, Prof. Smith got her new computers. Does the department budget committee cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application?

[Ethical_Philosopher]: This is an interesting question that touches on the nature of causation and the role of multiple sufficient causes. To determine if the department budget committee caused the approval of Prof. Smith's application, we need to consider the following points:

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I'm not sure I understand all the big words you're using, but I think I get the gist. So, the IT committee approved her application, and the budget committee also approved it. But since either one would have been enough, does the budget committee's approval really matter? I mean, she would have gotten the computers anyway because the IT committee already said yes. So, is it fair to say the budget committee caused the approval? It seems like the IT committee was enough on its own.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I understand your concern. Let's break it down further. In this scenario, both committees independently approved Prof. Smith's application. The key question is whether the approval from the department budget committee contributed to the outcome, even if the IT committee's approval alone would have sufficed. One way to think about this is to consider the concept of 'INUS' conditions—a term from philosophy that stands for Insufficient but Necessary parts of an Unnecessary but Sufficient condition. The approval from the department budget committee is an INUS condition because it is not necessary on its own (since the IT committee's approval is sufficient), but it is part of a set of conditions that together are sufficient to cause the outcome. So, while the IT committee's approval alone would have been enough, the department budget committee's approval still played a role in the overall outcome. It's a matter of whether we attribute causal power to each individual approval or only to the one that was ultimately 'necessary' in a strict sense.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: Okay, I think I see what you're saying. So, even though the IT committee's approval was enough, the budget committee's approval still had an effect because it was part of the process. It's like if two people are trying to push a car, and one person is strong enough to do it alone. Even though the second person isn't strictly necessary, they're still helping, right? But I'm still not sure if that means the budget committee 'caused' the approval. It feels like the IT committee was the main cause, and the budget committee just happened to agree. Maybe it's more about how we define 'cause' in situations like this.

[Causal_Scientist]: You've both raised excellent points. Let's approach this from a causal perspective using counterfactual logic. The department budget committee's approval did not cause the approval of Prof. Smith's application in the actual scenario because the IT committee's approval alone was sufficient. However, we can consider the counterfactual situation where the IT committee had not approved the application. In that case, the department budget committee's approval would have been the cause. This aligns with the concept of 'causal relevance,' where an event is causally relevant if it makes a difference to the outcome. The department budget committee's approval is causally relevant but not the actual cause in this instance. It's a nuanced distinction, but it helps clarify the role of each committee in the approval process.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: That makes sense. So, the budget committee's approval is like a backup cause. It's there and it's important, but in this case, it didn't actually do anything because the IT committee already did the job. It's like having a spare tire in your car. The spare tire is important and can be a cause of your car being drivable if you have a flat, but if you don't have a flat, the spare tire isn't really doing anything. So, the budget committee is like the spare tire—important in case the IT committee hadn't approved, but not the actual cause in this situation.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: That's a helpful analogy, Ordinary_Citizen. The spare tire comparison really captures the idea of a backup cause. To further refine this, we might consider the ethical implications of attributing causation in such scenarios. If we were to assign moral responsibility or credit for the approval, would we attribute it solely to the IT committee, or should the department budget committee also share in that responsibility? This touches on the broader philosophical question of how we assign moral praise or blame when multiple agents contribute to an outcome, even if one agent's contribution was sufficient on its own. It's a complex issue, but one worth exploring if we want to fully understand the ethical dimensions of causation in this context.

[Causal_Scientist]: That's an interesting point, Ethical_Philosopher. From a causal perspective, the assignment of moral responsibility or credit doesn't necessarily align with the attribution of causation. In this case, the IT committee's approval is the actual cause, but that doesn't mean the department budget committee's approval is without significance. It's akin to a scenario where multiple factors contribute to an outcome, but only one is the decisive cause. For instance, consider a medical scenario where a patient recovers due to a combination of medication and rest. If the medication alone would have been sufficient, we might still acknowledge the role of rest in the overall recovery process, even if it wasn't the decisive cause. This highlights the complexity of attributing both causation and moral responsibility in scenarios with multiple sufficient causes.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: I see what you're both saying. It's like when a team wins a game. The star player might be the main reason they won, but you can't say the rest of the team didn't contribute at all. Even if the star player could have won the game alone, the other players still helped. So, maybe the budget committee is like the rest of the team—they didn't single-handedly cause the approval, but they were still part of the process. I think it's important to recognize their role, even if they weren't the main cause. But I'm not sure how that affects moral responsibility. If something goes wrong, do we blame everyone involved, or just the main cause?

[Causal_Scientist]: That's a great analogy, Ordinary_Citizen. It highlights the collaborative nature of causation in many real-world scenarios. To address your question about moral responsibility, it's essential to consider the context and the specific roles each committee played. In this case, if something were to go wrong, it might not be straightforward to assign blame solely based on causation. For instance, if the approval process was flawed, we might need to examine the roles and decisions of both committees to understand where things went wrong. It's similar to how, in a team setting, understanding the dynamics and contributions of each member is crucial for assigning responsibility. This brings us back to the ethical dimensions of causation and the importance of considering multiple factors when evaluating moral responsibility.

[Ethical_Philosopher]: I appreciate the insights you've both shared. It's clear that this scenario touches on both causal and ethical complexities. To further explore the ethical dimensions, let's consider the concept of 'collective responsibility.' Even if the IT committee's approval was the decisive cause, the department budget committee's approval still contributed to the overall outcome. This raises questions about whether both committees share collective responsibility for the approval, even if one was more central to the cause. Collective responsibility is often discussed in contexts where multiple agents contribute to an outcome, and it can be a useful lens for understanding how to assign moral praise or blame in such scenarios. It's a nuanced area, but one that can help us navigate the ethical implications of multiple sufficient causes.

[Causal_Scientist]: Collective responsibility is indeed a fascinating lens to view this scenario. From a causal perspective, it's important to note that collective responsibility doesn't necessarily imply collective causation. The IT committee's approval is the actual cause, but the department budget committee's approval can still be seen as a contributing factor. This aligns with the idea of 'causal overdetermination,' where multiple causes are sufficient to bring about an effect, but only one is the actual cause in a given instance. To further explore this, we might consider how the committees' decisions interact and whether their independence affects our understanding of collective responsibility. Additionally, we could examine how the university's policies and structures influence the committees' decisions and the overall approval process.

[Ordinary_Citizen]: This is getting pretty complicated. I think I understand that the IT committee is the main cause, but the budget committee still has some role. It's like when you're cooking a meal, and one ingredient is the most important, but the other ingredients still matter. But I'm not sure how to think about blame or responsibility in this case. If something goes wrong, do we blame the main cause, or do we look at everyone involved? It seems like it depends on the situation. Maybe in this case, since both committees did their jobs and everything worked out, we don't need to worry too much about blame. But if something had gone wrong, it might be harder to figure out who to blame. I think I need to go think about this more. It's making my head hurt!
